subject: triangles
misconception_name: Does not know or misremembers triangle angle sum as 180°
explanation: Uses an incorrect total (90°, 270°, or other) instead of 180° for the sum of interior angles of a triangle.
why_students_think_this: Has not fully internalized the angle sum property. May confuse it with a right angle (90°) or with a full turn.
grade8_impact: Cannot solve any triangle angle problem. Foundational gap that blocks all triangle work.
grade12_impact: Will affect all geometry, trigonometry, and proof-based problems.
root_cause: Angle sum property not internalized; may have memorized it without understanding

subject: triangles
explanation: Subtracts angles from 360° instead of 180°, or treats the triangle as having angles summing to a full rotation.
why_students_think_this: 360° appears in many geometry contexts (angles around a point, quadrilateral exterior angles, full turn). This more prominent number gets applied to triangles.
grade8_impact: Third angle calculations will always be off by 180°. Algebraic triangle angle problems will be completely wrong.
grade12_impact: Geometry, trigonometry, and vector problems all depend on triangle angle sum.
root_cause: Overgeneralisation of 360° (a very frequently seen angle value) to triangles

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Adds sides instead of using Pythagoras (no squaring)
explanation: Computes a + b instead of √(a² + b²), or a − b instead of √(a² − b²), skipping the squaring and square root.
why_students_think_this: Addition is the simplest combination operation. Students who haven't internalized Pythagoras default to adding sides.
grade8_impact: Cannot apply Pythagoras in any form — identification, finding sides, or real-world applications.
grade12_impact: Affects distance formula, coordinate geometry, trigonometry, and physics (resultant vectors).
root_cause: Pythagoras not conceptualized as a relationship between areas of squares — just seen as a formula without geometric meaning

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Subtracts instead of adding when finding hypotenuse
explanation: Computes √(a² − b²) when asked for hypotenuse, instead of √(a² + b²).
why_students_think_this: May confuse the formula for finding a leg (subtract) with the formula for finding hypotenuse (add). Both operations exist in Pythagoras — students mix them up.
grade8_impact: Hypotenuse calculations will be consistently wrong. Will get correct answers only when finding a leg.
grade12_impact: Will confuse distance formula signs in coordinate geometry.
root_cause: Knows both addition and subtraction forms of Pythagoras but applies them inconsistently

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Applies Pythagoras to non-right triangles or misidentifies hypotenuse
explanation: Uses Pythagoras in a triangle that is not right-angled, or correctly identifies a right triangle but labels the hypotenuse incorrectly (as the shortest side, for example).
why_students_think_this: Knows Pythagoras involves three sides but hasn't internalized the condition (right angle) or the hypotenuse identification rule (opposite the right angle = longest side).
grade8_impact: Errors on all Pythagoras verification and application problems.
grade12_impact: Will make errors in trigonometry where SOH-CAH-TOA requires correctly identifying hypotenuse.
root_cause: Formula memorized without understanding the geometric conditions for its application

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Confuses triangle types (equilateral, isosceles, scalene, right, obtuse, acute)
explanation: Cannot correctly identify or apply the properties of different triangle types. For example, thinks equilateral means only sides are equal (not angles), or thinks isosceles means right-angled.
why_students_think_this: Six triangle types overlap (a triangle can be both right and isosceles). Students who haven't built a systematic classification framework get confused.
grade8_impact: Cannot use specific triangle properties to solve angle and side problems.
grade12_impact: Trigonometry and coordinate geometry rely on identifying triangle types.
root_cause: Triangle classification taught as a naming exercise rather than a properties-based framework

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Thinks equilateral refers only to equal sides, not equal angles
explanation: Knows that equilateral means all sides are equal but doesn't know this implies all angles are also equal (60° each).
why_students_think_this: 'Equilateral' literally means 'equal sides'. Students who learned the word meaning but not the geometric implication miss the angle consequence.
grade8_impact: Cannot determine angles of equilateral triangles; cannot identify impossible triangle angle combinations.
grade12_impact: Trigonometric values for 60° are derived from equilateral triangles — this misconception blocks that derivation.
root_cause: Has not been shown the geometric proof that equal sides imply equal angles in a triangle

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Confuses exterior angle theorem with supplementary angle rule
explanation: Applies the supplementary rule (interior + exterior = 180°) instead of the exterior angle theorem (exterior = sum of two non-adjacent interior angles).
why_students_think_this: The supplementary relationship is a simpler and more familiar rule. Students default to it, especially when the exterior angle theorem requires identifying the two non-adjacent angles.
grade8_impact: Cannot find angles using exterior angle theorem. Gets incorrect answers for all such problems.
grade12_impact: Exterior angle theorem is used in many proofs and circle theorem derivations.
root_cause: Exterior angle theorem not consolidated; supplements rule is more strongly memorized

subject: triangles
explanation: Thinks the altitude bisects the base (making it also a median), or cannot distinguish between the perpendicular from a vertex and the line to the midpoint.
why_students_think_this: In an equilateral or isosceles triangle with the apex above the midpoint, altitude and median coincide. Students generalize this special case to all triangles.
grade8_impact: Errors in area calculation (uses incorrect height) and in identifying triangle properties.
grade12_impact: Centroid, orthocentre, and related triangle centres require distinguishing altitude, median, and angle bisector.
root_cause: Over-generalisation from the special case (equilateral/isosceles) where altitude = median

subject: triangles
explanation: Thinks the altitude from a vertex also bisects the angle at that vertex.
why_students_think_this: In equilateral and isosceles triangles, altitude, angle bisector, and median from the apex all coincide. Students generalize to all triangles.
grade8_impact: Cannot solve problems where altitude and angle bisector are used as distinct constructions.
grade12_impact: All triangle centre problems (incenter, orthocenter) depend on this distinction.
root_cause: Over-generalisation from symmetric triangles to all triangles

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Uses incorrect base-height pair in triangle area formula
explanation: Selects a side that is not the base, or uses a slant side instead of the perpendicular height, or uses two sides that are not a base-height pair.
why_students_think_this: Area = ½ × base × height is learned but 'base' and 'height' aren't understood as perpendicular pair — any two sides can seem equally valid.
grade8_impact: Incorrect area calculations for all non-standard triangle orientations.
grade12_impact: Will affect integration (areas under curves) and vector cross products.
root_cause: Base and height not understood as a perpendicular pair — any two sides seem equivalent

subject: triangles
misconception_name: Does not check whether angle combination is valid for a triangle
explanation: Accepts angle combinations that are geometrically impossible (e.g., two obtuse angles, angles summing to more than 180°) without recognizing the contradiction.
why_students_think_this: Has not internalized the constraint that all triangle angles must be positive and sum to 180°. Treats each angle as independent.
grade8_impact: Cannot evaluate whether a given triangle description is valid or impossible.
grade12_impact: Proof-based geometry requires recognizing impossible configurations.
root_cause: Angle sum rule known procedurally but not used as a constraint on possible angle values
